coredata.dc:description: With the explosive development of research on phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs), it is of great significance to explore their mechanisms and establish the relationship among mechanism–structure–property. However, owing to the diversity of carbon dot structure, there is still no definite and unified model to describe them. In this review, the phosphorescent emission mechanism (including the promotion of ISC process and the stabilization of triplet exciton) and the factors affecting the property of CDs (including the carbon core state, surface state, the synergistic effect of the above-mentioned, and environmental factors) are summarized and elaborated. Then, the basic principles of the synthesis of modulated phosphorescent CDs are also summarized and their application fields are introduced. The full text is committed to finding out the shortcomings of current research through reference, finally summarizing problems, and putting forward prospects.

head.ce:title: Phosphorescence of Carbon Dot: The Intrinsic Mechanism and Recent Progress

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of New Carbon Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of New Carbon Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the explosive development of research on phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs), it is of great significance to explore their mechanisms and establish the relationship among mechanism–structure–property. However, owing to the diversity of carbon dot structure, there is still no definite and unified model to describe them. In this review, the phosphorescent emission mechanism (including the promotion of ISC process and the stabilization of triplet exciton) and the factors affecting the property of CDs (including the carbon core state, surface state, the synergistic effect of the above-mentioned, and environmental factors) are summarized and elaborated. Then, the basic principles of the synthesis of modulated phosphorescent CDs are also summarized and their application fields are introduced. The full text is committed to finding out the shortcomings of current research through reference, finally summarizing problems, and putting forward prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Phosphorescence is commonly found in metal complexes and inorganic compounds [1,2], and corresponds to the radiative decay of an excited triplet state. So there are some unique characteristics, such as long lifetime and large Stokes shift. However, an excited triplet state in organic compounds can easily be released non-radiatively through a thermal collisional process. Therefore, their phosphorescence is generally observed in inert gases or at extremely low temperatures [3,4]. In the past, it was long believed that organic compounds could not generate room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) until a bright blue-to-green phosphorescent emission with a visible duration up to 23 s from tetraphenylmethane and tetraphenylsilane derivatives in crystalline state was observed by Clapp [5] in 1939. In 1978, Bilen et al. [6] found that organic compounds such as crystalline carbazole, dibenzothiophene, dibenzofuran, and triphenylene can also generate RTP, which proved that pure organic molecules can generate RTP. Typical energy transfer processes in organic molecules are described in Figure 2a
. Meanwhile, the intrinsic mechanism of phosphorescence can be intuitively illustrated by the Jablonski diagram (Figure 2b). Being excited by incident light, the electrons in ground state experience a transition from the ground state (S0) to the excited singlet state (Sn) [7]. Then, through the process of non-radiative energy transfer, the exciton at energy levels Sn in the excited singlet state transits to the lowest energy state S1. The exciton at S1 returns directly to S0 through a radiative process called fluorescent process, which is a spin-allowed process. The exciton in Sn can cross to the excited triplet state (Tn) through an intersystem crossing (ISC) process, but this is a spin-forbidden process [8]. After that, the exciton can transit to the lowest excited triplet state (T1) through a non-radiative process. Finally, the exciton at T1 returns directly to S0 through a radiation process called phosphorescence, which is also a spin-forbidden process. Thus, phosphorescence is intrinsically slower than the spin-allowed fluorescence process with longer lifetime [9]. Consequently, there are two main issues needing to be solved to achieve phosphorescence:
1
The spin-forbidden ISC should be broken to enable exciton to cross to the excited triplet state.
2
It is necessary to construct a rigid microenvironment to suppress molecular vibration to reduce non-radiative relaxation and to shield quenching elements so as to stabilize excited triplet excitons.

Compared with traditional organic dyes, inorganic compounds, and perovskite quantum dots, CDs as a new type of phosphorescent nanomaterial have a large number of characteristics such as broad emission, narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM), long lifetime, high stability, high dispersion rate, and low toxicity [10–13]. At present, gratifying achievements have been achieved in anti-counterfeiting [14–16], bioimaging [17,18], sensing [19–24], and LED lighting [25–29]. Therefore, it is of great research value and potential to explore the phosphorescence mechanism of CDs and learn to regulate the optical properties of CDs through accurate structural design. With the expansion of the types of CDs (Figure 3 ), the field of phosphorescent CDs is developing faster and faster [30]. However, a consensus is yet to be achieved on the phosphorescent mechanism and structure–property relationship of CDs. This review gives a summary of recent research progress of phosphorescent CDs by combining two more mature fields of phosphorescent organic luminogens and fluorescent CDs, and building the relationship among material structure, property, and application, so as to guide the preparation and regulation, and enrich their application, as shown in Figure 1.

For CDs (similar to other organic luminogens), it is extremely challenging to achieve RTP with long lifetime and high quantum yield owing mainly to the following issues. First, compared with the case in traditional semiconductor quantum dots and inorganic compounds, spin-forbiddance cannot be effectively overcome in organic materials [31]. Second, an excited triplet state can be easily deactivated by intramolecular vibration as a non-radiative process in organic molecules because of their irregular configuration compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and inorganic crystal. Meanwhile, the instability of the excited triplet state means that it is easily subjected to quenching by a very low concentration of quenching elements, including impurities, oxygen, and humidity, as a result of triplet energy migration.

To facilitate the ISC process, a common strategy is to add or introduce spin–orbital coupling (SOC) matrix elements into the π conjugated systems [32], such as metal, halogen, and the unit with n electrons (heteroatoms like N, P, S, and carbonyl etc.), which can enhance the SOC effect. The role of introducing these units is usually closely related to the heavy-atom effect, the involved orbital type and symmetry, as well as the relative energy levels [33]. According to the El-Sayed rule [34,35], the rates of radiationless ISC transitions of 1(π, π*)→3(n, π*) type are higher than those of ISC for 1(π, π*)→3(π, π*) transitions. And similarly, the 1(n, π*)→3(π, π*) transitions are usually faster than the 1(n, π*)→3(n, π*) transitions. This is why heteroatoms, as n electron sources, are often introduced into π conjugated systems to produce n-π* transitions. As a common n electron source, N has been widely used to induce phosphorescence.

Relevant studies have shown that both the doping of graphitic nitrogen in carbon core state and the doping of C=N functional group in surface state contribute to phosphorescence. As indicated in Figure 4
[36], the outer-shell electron configurations of carbon and nitrogen atom orbitals are 2s22p2 and 2s22p3, and one s orbital and two p orbitals can form three sp2 hybrid orbitals in CNDs. For the nitrogen atoms within CNDs, the three sp2 hybrid orbitals are not equivalent, which leads to a lone pair of electrons residing in one of the sp2 hybrid orbitals. This lone pair of electrons is not involved in the π-electron system, thus strengthening SOC because of the inversion asymmetry band structure, which is beneficial to the realization of phosphorescence. First-principles calculations demonstrate that the electron transition from the px to the sp2 orbital of the N atoms within CNDs can generate one-unit orbital angular momentum, providing a driving force for the triplet exciton population of CNDs.

As indicated in Figure 5a , graphitic nitrogen plays an important role in the red phosphorescence emission dominated by carbon core state [37]. To develop a deeper understanding of the effect of the N forms (graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N) in the sp2 conjugated domain on PL mechanism, Wei et al. investigated the geometries of the first singlet excited state of cyclopenta [cd] pyrene systems as the N-doped models. Four aromatic cycles and N atoms are substituted at different positions to mimic the nature of the CDs’ carbon core structure. Figure 5b reveals the N electrons of graphitic N participating in π electron delocalization in the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the whole molecule. The N electrons of pyridinic N are not delocalized, whereas the N electrons of pyrrolic N are delocalized partly in the LUMO orbitals. Different from that of the parent system (the HOMO–LUMO gap is 5.30 eV), the HOMO-LUMO gaps of the pyrrolic N and pyridinic N systems are very similar. But a remarkable decrease of the gap (3.03 eV) is achieved through graphittic N doping, which is conducive to the emission of long wavelength phosphorescent luminescence (PL). In the meantime, the participation of graphitic N in the p-π* conjugated system leads to HOMO and LUMO energy level splitting. The splitting can lead to some energy level crossings, thus generating effective ISC and form small energy gap. Then, Wei et al. synthesized graphitic N doped CDs with citric acid and urea, then achieved red phosphorescence by encapsulating them in PVA matrix. (Figure 5a)

Besides N, a lot of other heteroatoms have been used to control the emission of phosphorescence. Su et al. realized the control of phosphorescence by changing the protonation and deprotonation of P–O bond on the surface of CD through pH control [15]. N, S co-doped phosphorescent CDs were prepared by Sudhaker P. et al. [38] to obtain RTP. Xu et al. [39] also achieved the regulation of the phosphorescent property of B-doped CDs. In addition to the above function, they can always be the site of hydrogen bond, covalent bond, and other bonds, so they can assist in building rigid environment.

Another more usual unit used to facilitate the ISC process is the introduction of carboxyl group, which has become a consensus in the field to realize the phosphorescence of CDs. Sun et al. [40] synthesized a series of afterglow emission CDs from a series of benzoic acid derivatives with different carboxyl substituents and ethylenediamine. RTP and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) conversion are realized by using different carboxyl substitution positions to regulate the energy gap. In addition to doping units that can promote ISC process into CDs, charge-transfer state (CT state)/charge-separation state (CSS state) can also be established between systems through different components to emit phosphorescence. As shown in Figure 6
[41], m-CDs@CA exciplex is formed after m-CDs are embedded into cyanuric acid matrix. After excitation, a CT state is formed through energy transfer. The formation of the CT state leads to the energy level splitting of the CDs and the matrix, thus narrowing the energy gap. The splitting may cause the possible cross between energy levels, which inturn keenly promotes the ISC process, thus enhancing the emission of phosphorescence.

In addition, according to the heavy atom effect, the introduction of both internal and external halogen atoms can effectively enhance the ISC process [42,43]. This process takes effect by enhancing the SOC between excited electrons. And by doping halogen, the quantum yield of phosphorescence can be regulated. Liu et al. [44] used chlorinated derivatives of p-benzoquinone and ethylenediamine as raw materials to synthesize a series of CDs (CDs-1–3) with different chlorine content from 6.0% to 9.3% (Figure 7 ). Their quantum yield of phosphorescence increased regularly with the increase of chlorine content, being 1.61%, 1.93%, and 2.93% for CDs-1–3, respectively. Interestingly, the authors verified that although bromine and iodine can produce stronger heavy atom effect in the process of synthesizing halogenated phosphorescent CDs through bottom-up routes, the departure effect of bromine and iodine is more obvious under normal CDs synthesis conditions, leading to the low halogen content of the final product. Therefore, chlorine doping may be the best choice for CDs halogen doping. It is worth noting that fluorine is not usually doped as heavy atom, as its relatively light nucleus cannot play its heavy atom effect well, but its high stability is often used for self-protection [45].

Therefore, for phosphorescent CDs, reasonable structure design and the introduction of special intermolecular interaction can effectively enhance the ISC effect and greatly increase the number of excited triplet excitons. Subsequently, according to the Jablonski diagram, it is necessary to minimize the adverse non-radiative decay and suppress the exciton quenching effect to stabilize the excited triplet state. Generally, this process can be realized by building a rigid microenvironment for self-protection or by using an external rigid environment to form a host–guest system for confinement.

In the design of phosphorescent CDs, the preferred method to minimize non-radiative transitions and suppress exciton quenching effects is to build a rigid microenvironment for self-protection, or to form a host–guest system by virtue of external rigid environment. For self-protection, basically, the quenching effect of exciton quenching agents such as oxygen and water is shielded by the stable surface formed with inert functional groups on the surface and strong interaction. Fluorine atom, Si–O, and C–B [46] etc. are frequently used inert functional groups. Long et al. [47] constructed and synthesized F, N co-doped CDs (Figure 8a ). A large amount of C–F on the surface of the CDs plays the role of shielding quenching agent through steric hindrance. The large endothermic transition at 10–80°C in DSC test proved that a large number of intra- or inter-dot hydrogen bonds are formed in the CDs after the introduction of F atom, and the existence of hydrogen bonds also effectively inhibited the oxygen quenching of RTP. Liu et al. [17] constructed Si, N, P co-doped CDs, with a large number of Si–O bonds on their surfaces. The obtained CDs are wrapped by SiO2 shells, effectively shielding the quenching of water and oxygen (Figure 8b, c). Through this strategy, phosphorescent CDs stably existing in aqueous solutions were obtained. In addition to the intentional use of inert surface functional groups, another mainstream self-protection mechanism is using strong interactions to suppress molecular vibration, and at the same time using relatively tight cores formed by strong interactions and relatively inert hydroxyl functional groups on the surface to shield quenching agents. At present, there are several popular theories for this type of self-protection mechanism, including the so-called cross-linking enhanced emission effect (CEE effect), cluster induced emission (CIE), polymerization induced emission (PIE), and aggregation induced emission (AIE). All of these explanations take advantage of the configuration and conformation changes caused by strong interactions. In recent years, polymer carbon dots (PDs) and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) have become brilliant in this direction [48–50]. For example, the common fluorescent polymer carbon dots synthesized by ethylenediamine and phosphoric acid exhibit the ultralong RTP (URTP) effect after further heat treatment, with a phosphorescent duration of 10 s and a lifetime of 1.39 s [51] (Figure 8d). The conversion from fluorescence to phosphorescence is proved to be the result of heating to promote the self-crosslink and fixation of polymer CDs. Self-crosslink can form a large number of intra-dot hydrogen bonds, which makes the CDs form a denser core. The research of Xia et al. [52,53] indicated that the hydrophilicity of the functional groups on the polymer chain is one of the important factors promoting the formation of polymer carbon dots. After self-crosslinking and generating a large number of intra-dot hydrogen bonds, CDs with more stable phosphorescent emission can be formed (Figure 8e).

At present, the more universally and commonly used measure to stabilize triplet exciton is to build a host–guest system by virtue of external rigid environment. Mainly, the host matrix should be compatible with guest CDs. The excited triplet and singlet energy levels of host material should be always higher than those of guest phosphorescence CDs to prevent energy back transfer from the guest light-emitting CDs to the non-light-emitting host material [54]. Encapsulating CDs into various host materials is to use the regular structure of the matrix itself for spatial confinement or to use the interaction between CDs and the matrix to suppress the molecular vibration of CDs to decrease the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons. As shown in Figure 9a , Wang et al. [55,56] uniformly confined CDs into an inorganic salt crystal through molten salt method. The regular structure of inorganic salt crystal greatly inhibits the non-radiative transition of excitons. At the same time, metal ions such as sodium and magnesium and halogen ions such as chlorine ion in inorganic salt crystals can decrease ΔE ST and enhance the role of spin–orbital coupling to promote the ISC process. Inorganic salt crystals have been widely investigated to confine CDs to achieve RTP of CDs. Similarly, silica gel [57], mesoporous silica, and even filter paper [58] have been used to achieve RTP of CDs (Figure 9b, c). In the system CDs@MOF [59], the secondary structural unit of zinc carboxylate is formed on the surface of CDs owing to the affinity between the functional groups on the surface of CDs and the carboxylate groups of organic bridging molecules. These paddle–wheel dinuclear secondary building units are bridged by TPA ligands, forming two-dimensional layers that are stacked in an offset fashion and producing three-dimensional MOFs embedding CDs. The preparation of phosphorescent CDs with multi-wavelength and adjustable lifetime was achieved by using the space-confinement of MOF crystal and the weak coordination between them (Figure 9d, e).

To a certain extent, the strength of the interaction between CDs and matrices determines the performance of RTP. In the work designed by Jiang et al. [41], CDs were embedded into cyanuric acid matrix to obtain phosphorescence with ultralong lifetime (Figure 10a–c ). Excellent RTP performance was realized when CDs were crystallized and precipitated by co-heat treatment with CA, while no RTP was obtained when CDs were crystallized and precipitated by co-crystallization with CA. Intrinsically, the reason is that the covalent bond formed between the CDs and CA can better suppress the molecular vibration of the CDs. Therefore, compared with other weak interaction, covalent action is more suited to confine the CDs to obtain RTP. The main host materials using covalent action include amorphous polymer matrix, such as PVA [60] and PAM [16], and also include crystalline matrices, such as boric acid [61,62] and cyanuric acid [63]. It is worth mentioning that ionic bond, as a relatively stronger interaction than covalent bond, will have a greater application potential in organic phosphorescence in the future. Ding et al. [64] realized the preparation of citric acid-derived CDs confined in situ in NaOH crystals by microwave assisted hydrothermal method (Figure 10d). The method is simple and fast, and suitable for batch preparation. The synthesized CDs have tunable phosphorescent lifetime and wavelength. The preparation of full-color phosphorescent CDs can be achieved by adjusting the ratio of CA to NaOH. With the reduction of NaOH content, the phosphorescent wavelength gradually shifts from 483 to 635 nm, and the phosphorescent lifetime decreases from 389 to 58 ms. As shown in Figure 10e, the CDs gradually aggregate as NaOH addition decreases. Therefore, owing to the π-π stacking effect and non-radiative energy transfer (mainly triplet state–triplet state annihilation) between CDs in the NaOH matrix, the PL emission is red shifted. In the field of organic phosphorescent small molecules, Ye et al. [65] synthesized pure blue phosphorescent materials with phosphorescent quantum yield up to 96.5% by the methods of ionic crystal confinement, and realized full-color phosphorescent emission by adjusting host guest materials. The future exploration in ionic bond is worth looking forward to.

With the deepening of research, it has been found that the compatibility of matrix structure with CDs imposes meaningful impact on RTP property. As shown in Figure 10f, g [61], the fracture and fragmentation caused by grinding the CDs embedded into boric acid matrix induce the transformation of high-energy amorphous state into low-energy crystalline state, thus realizing the preparation of carbon dot materials with up to 48% phosphorescence quantum yield. In order to better consult and learn from previous work in the future, the mainstream materials used at present are summarized in Table 1 .

In addition to the methods mentioned above, the formation of inter- or intramolecular charge transfer state (CT state) also contributes to the phosphorescence in pure organic small molecules (Figure 11 ) [66–68]. The formation of charge transfer states usually involves the separation of HOMO energy levels and LUMO energy levels. Therefore, when energy level transitions occur, the configuration of molecule itself will not change much, so reducing the non-radiative energy transitions caused by the rearrangement of electron cloud.

Besides the usual role of suppressing non-radiative transitions, some particular functions belonging to host matrix materials can derive more special properties. Phosphorescent CDs obtained by embedding m-CDs synthesized from o-phenylenediamine into cyanuric acid matrix (m-CDs@CA) achieve an ultralong phosphorescence lifetime up to 1 h, which was realized for the first time (Figure 10b, c). After a short microwave treatment, m-CDs were embedded into CA. They were bridged in the form of covalent bonds to form excimer complexes. After being excited, they produce charge-separated states (CSS), which would slowly recombine in the next 2 hours. The slow recombination of the charge-separated state is the reason for the ultralong phosphorescent lifetime. The duration of phosphorescent lifetime is dependent on excitation time, sample temperature, and the content of embedded m-CDs (Figure 6). As shown in Figure 12
[69], the CDs confined by COF (CODF) were synthesized from 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride, 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid, and guanidinium dihydrogen phosphate. The π-π conjugation through the regular parallel arrangement (0°) of perylene–perylene molecules forms π channels in CODF chain. The π channels have higher hole transport stability than perylene–perylene molecules with a certain angle arrangement. CODF material reached higher charge mobility through the transport of holes compounded with electrons captured in CDs. The electron and hole mobility values are up to 1.26×10−3 and 3.15×10−3, respectively, among which the hole transfer rate is 2–3 orders higher than that of previously reported CDs, and the electron transfer rate is even equivalent to electron transfer materials.

In addition to the various methods introduced above to achieve RTP, a vital part of the research on the phosphorescence emission of traditional organic small molecule is the exploration of the influence of the stacking form (aggregation form) of molecules in crystalline state on phosphorescent emission. Among them, the electron delocalization system is extended by intermolecular coupling to stabilize the triplet exciton. In general, the face-to-face parallel structure with strong π-π interaction is considered to be one of the most favorable packaging modes. At present, it has been gradually clarified that the H-aggregation with short distance between conjugated systems and large overlap at a short distance between adjacent molecules can generate stronger binding energy than the J-aggregation with long distance between conjugated systems [70–72]. The possibility of non-radiative transition is weakened because the molecular motion is repressed by strong intermolecular interaction [73]. During the research on RTP of pure organic small molecules, it was found that the molecule with electron-withdrawing substituents would reduce the electron density of corresponding benzene ring owing to the inductive effect of halogen atoms, which would reduce the π-π repulsion between the two rings involved, resulting in shorter distance and stronger π-π interaction. However, the configuration of traditional CDs is obviously different from that of pure organic small molecules, which is characterized by graphitized carbon cores inside and rich functional groups on the surface. It is speculated that the strength of the interlayer interaction of crystalline graphite may also have an important impact on the phosphorescent emission of CDs. Current researches have also proved that electron-pushing group or electron-withdrawing group has an important impact on the phosphorescence of CDs [74]. It is expected that in the future, all scientific researchers can do follow-up research on the variation of phosphorescent emission from the perspective of the interaction between graphitic layers in CDs. The detailed study of RTP depending on electronic properties will provide the relationship among structure, stacking mode, and RTP property, which can guide structural design to achieve excellent RTP in carbon dot materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors dominating emission of phosphorescent CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The quantum confinement effect or size effect, which is a widely recognized carbon dot photoluminescence mechanism, is always referred to as the phenomenon that occurs when the particle size drops below a certain value. The electronic energy level near the Fermi level changes from quasi-continuous to discrete, that is, the energy level splits or the energy gap widens, in which "size" is referred to as the sp2 (graphene) conjugated domain, that is, the effective conjugated length rather than the actual particle size [36,75]. For CDs, with the increase of "size", the band gap decreases, causing a corresponding increase in PL emission wavelength. The increase in the interior graphene domain of CDs results in a more delocalization of electrons and a more even distribution of energy, which in turn decreases the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO. This model is suitable for explaining the photoluminescence of the CDs with clear lattice structures or a high degree of graphitization.

Ding et al. [75] used citric acid and boric acid to synthesize a series of phosphorescent carbon dot composites with different phosphorescent emission wavelengths. The average particle size of CDs in the composite increases with the increase of CA feeding and temperature. In the HADDF-STEM and TEM images, the CDs show highly crystalline structures, and the spacing of the lattice stripes corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite; and with the increase of the particle size of CDs, the color of phosphorescence changes from blue to red (Figure 13a–e ). As indicated in Figure 13f, in order to achieve ultraviolet phosphorescent emission of CDs, the size of CDs is customized by NaCNO crystals [36]. With the increase of temperature, the Na2CO3 crystal around CDs gradually transforms into NaCNO, and the size of CDs also changes from >100 nm down until the ultraviolet phosphorescent emission is realized at 3.6 nm. The calculations based on the first principles also prove that there are two possible configurations with smaller size in the CDs with melamine as phosphorescence emitting unit. Meanwhile, the highly overlapping LUMO and HOMO energy levels lead to the effective recombination of electrons and holes, which endows CDs with efficient ultraviolet phosphorescent ability. Besides, the conjugation degree of CDs is not only related to the size of conjugated domain, but also affected by the type of functional group. The steric hindrance effect of functional group and the introduction of electron-withdrawing group weaken the conjugation effect, leading to the blue shift of luminescence [74].

In the case that the "conjugation effect" is not applicable, the "surface state" model is proposed to explain the luminescence sites changing with electronic energy level. Two surface configurations are mainly considered here, namely, surface configuration and doping atoms. The former can be understood as a wide surface structure type, such as polymer chains, functional groups (different oxidation degrees), defects, and edge states. The latter involves heteroatoms such as nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, fluorine, etc. A lot of studies have shown that regulating the surface state to adjust the energy structure of CD affects their phosphorescent property.

With the development of CDs, the concept of CDs continues to be expanded. A variety of detailed classifications of PDs, CPDs, CQDs, and GQDs have evolved. There is even no such thing as carbon crystal structure in some PDs and CPDs, and therefore, the related photoluminescence can no longer be explained by the "conjugate effect". Consequently, the "surface state" model is proposed to fill the theoretical gap. The reason why the emission sites change with electron energy level has been well explained by the surface model.

Generally, surface state is understood as surface configuration, such as polymer, functional group (different oxidation degree), defect, and edge state. The change of surface configuration may change the electronic energy levels or luminescence sites of CDs, or even induce the emission of CDs that do not emit before.

Normally, in the phosphorescent emission process of CDs, the increase of oxidation degree of CDs may cause the phosphorescent wavelength to red shift. In a reported system of boric acid and citric acid [75], with the increase of citric acid feeding and temperature, the degree of oxidation (C=O content) of CDs in B2O3 matrix increases from 28.16% to 58.16% (Figure 13a). In CDs, the orbital configuration changes caused by C=O and C=N bonds are beneficial to RTP, while carboxyl groups have been proved to be important SOC moieties. It is interesting that their protonation and deprotonation (ionized and non-ionized states) often affect their RTP properties. Li et al. [76] effectively realized dynamically adjustable phosphorescent color from CDs@paper through surface ionization engineering. Phenol hydroxyl ionization on CDs can improve ISC, and negatively charged surface ionized groups significantly passivate surface defects and reduce non-radiative transitions, thus activating the blue-phosphorescent emission related to surface state. Combined with the yellow-green phosphorescence related to the carbon core state, the excited/time-dependent phosphorescence of CDs treated with alkali (Na2CO3) on paper changes from yellow green to blue. In addition, NaHCO3 is converted to Na2CO3 under heat treatment, so dynamic color changing phosphorescence can also be activated by heat treating the CDs@paper treated by NaHCO3 (Figure 14 ).

Similar to the case of carboxyl group, under acidic conditions, the strong vibration coupling of P=O…H hydrogen bond may lead to the formation of stronger conformational rigidity and wider energy level of P-CDs, thus inhibiting RTP from being quenched [15]. In contrast, the hydrogen bond is destroyed owing to the deprotonation of P–O bond under alkaline conditions, which can reduce the vibration coupling and narrow the energy level. At the same time, with the increase of pH value, the n-π* transition of C=N bond disappears, leading to the triplet state annihilation. This series of processes may lead to the electrostatic doping/charging of P-CDs and the change in their Fermi energy levels. It is interesting that the average phosphorescent lifetime of P-CDs is 822.69 ms (pH=2.95), 820.80 ms (pH=7.00) and 795.24 ms (pH=11.35), which are also related to the surface state and defect state in the carrier recombination process. The relationship between RTP and defect state can be observed intuitively through ESR and other methods [74].

Doping heteroatoms in CDs, such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, fluorine, and some heavy atoms, is also a common method to change the electronic structure of CDs. Although the doping position may not be on the "surface", it can change the electronic structure of CDs, so it is classified as a surface technology.

In addition to the atomic doping mentioned above to facilitate ISC process, here we mainly discuss the influence of atomic doping on luminescent properties, of which the law is also suitable for phosphorescent luminescence. Nitrogen and sulfur are common dopants, in which the atomic radius of the former is similar to that of carbon, while the electronegativity of the latter is similar [77]. Their influence on the quantum yield (QY) and excitation dependence of CDs has gone deeply into the research by Dong et al. [78]. The authors have classified CDs as follows. The first is O-doped "O-CDs", which are characterized by low QY values, excitation-dependent emission, and a broad UV–vis absorption band. The second is N-doped ‘‘N-CDs”, which are characterized by higher QY values, similar excitation dependence, and wide spectra. The last is N, S co-doped "N, S-CDs", which are characterized by high QY values and excitation-independent emission. Various surface states ("O states") of O-CDs are relevant to the excitation dependence and broad absorption band (Figure 15a ). Nitrogen-doping introduces a new type of surface state (the "N state"), which affects the electron clouds so as to produce high radiative recombination yields. The further addition of S atoms seems to suppress O-states and enhance N-states, which generates stronger excitation-independent photoluminescence emissions. Song et al. [79] proposed a homologous mechanism for N-doping enhanced by S-doping.

As to heavy-element doping, Yang et al. [80] proposed a theory on the basis of electron donors and acceptors as related to electronegativity. As indicated in Figure 15b, the doping of S or Se into CDs red-shifts their photoluminescence spectra while N-doping blue-shifts them, and the QY values of heavy-element-doped CDs are dependent on the electronegativity of heteroatoms. This is attributed to electron acceptors such as N promoting electron transfer (increasing QY), and electron donors such as S and Se reducing electron transfer (reducing QY to below that of N-CDs). It was also found that a large number of oxygen-containing groups in O-CDs lead to non-radiative coupling of localized electron–hole pairs, suppressing radiative transition, thus accounting for the doping with high-electronegativity oxygen leading to very low QY values (similar to Figure 15a).

The phosphorescence of CDs is not often determined solely by a single carbon core state or single surface state, but more often, it is shown as a "synergistic effect" of mutual coordination and interaction. Understanding this synergy is of great significance for understanding the RTP mechanism of CDs. In the process of deprotonation of carboxyl group [25,81], the increase of negative surface charge not only makes the Fermi level shift, but also inhibits the electronic transition of some emission centers by occupying the energy level. In addition, the deprotonation of carboxyl group weakens the electron absorption of carboxyl group to carbon core, and then increases the degree of conjugation between carboxyl group and carbon core. The large degree of conjugation is more conducive to the generation of excited electrons. The increase of the number of excited electrons and the decrease of surface defects lead to the enhancement of RTP (Figure 16a, b ). As shown in Figure 16c, the phosphorescence of CDs designed by Li et al. [76] changes from the original pure yellowish green to yellowish green–blue dynamic phosphorescence after alkali treatment. It is due to the activation of blue RTP emission sites induced by the deprotonation of surface carboxyl group, and the yellowish green phosphorescence dominated by carbon core state is also enhanced by the deprotonation of surface carboxyl group.

Besides the synergism of surface state and carbon core state at microscopic level such as electronic energy level, the synergism also includes that of multiple light-emitting sites. Tan et al. [82] used levofloxacin as raw material to prepare dynamic color-changing phosphorescent CDs, which display green phosphorescent emission at 365 nm excitation wavelength, orange to green phosphorescent emission at 395 nm excitation wavelength, and pure red phosphorescent emission at 425 nm excitation wavelength. Through the surface reduction treatment with NaBH4, the green and red phosphorescence originates respectively from the high-energy N-related triplet state and surface oxide-related triplet state. At 395 nm excitation wavelength, the double excitation sites are activated, so the red and green luminescence is mixed to form orange luminescence. Among that, the red phosphorescence with a shorter lifetime disappears early, which induces the phosphorescence change from orange to green after a period of time (Figure 17 ). The synergy between these different light-emitting sites has a huge impact on the RTP emission of CDs and even the whole photoluminescence process, which constitutes the rich change in the photoluminescence of CDs.

The phosphorescent properties of CDs not only depend on their inherent characteristics, but also are often affected by external environment, such as molecular state, solvent effect, pH, temperature, and incident ray.

Dialysis is a common method for purification of CDs. It is used to separate "impurities" from CDs by taking advantage of their different sizes. During dialysis, similar luminescence phenomena are occasionally found in dialysate and carbon spot. It is speculated that the light emission sites of CDs may originate from small molecular states independent of the existence of carbon core and physically attaching to the surface of CDs. Song et al. [83] proved that in the CDs prepared by CA and EDA, excitation-dependent carbon core and excitation-independent small molecule fluorescence group IPCA coexist (Figure 18a ). In addition to the attached states of these small molecules that emit light independent of carbon core, another type of small molecule state luminescence mechanism is the high-performance luminescence achieved through the synergistic effect of the surface-attached small molecule state and carbon core state.

In the synthesis of phosphorescent CDs prepared by Bai et al. [84] using OPD as raw material and AlCl3·6H2O as catalyst through solvent-free one-step method, AlCl3·6H2O not only acts as catalyst, but also remains as trace Al3+ after reaction, playing a role in electron transfer, and thus is one of the important factors causing RTP. After washing and purification with water, the elimination of Al3+ and the destruction of hydrogen bond lead to the disappearance of phosphorescence. In phosphorescent CDs with inorganic crystal materials as matrix [56,85], the metal elements in inorganic crystals are often regarded as efficient SOC matrix units. So the metal elements attached to CDs can also be regarded as a kind of molecular state.

The influence of solvent on the luminescence of CDs has been widely studied. The solvent relaxation effect is the main one. Solvent relaxation is one type of solvent effect. Kasha–Vavilov rule points out that for molecules with multiple states, photons can only be emitted from the lowest excited state. These photons can be emitted in the form of fluorescence or phosphorescence. Therefore, the wavelength of emitted light is independent of the wavelength of excited light. Khan et al. [86] used time-resolved emission to reveal excitation-dependent fluorescence. As indicated in Figure 18b, after absorption of light (10−15 s), two nonradiative processes—vibrational relaxation (10−9 s) and solvent relaxation—take place to the band edge, followed by radiative fluorescence (10−9 s). The authors came to a conclusion that rapid solvation (10−12 s) leads to complete relaxation before fluorescence decay (10−9 s), which causes excitation-independent fluorescence. While solvation dynamics slows down to fluorescence decay (10−9 s), a set of ensemble of substates forms, causing excitation-dependent fluorescence. According to the Lippert–Mataga equation [87], the greater the polarity of solvent, the greater the red shift. Arshad et al. [88] reached similar conclusions through analysis of emission spectra of CDs dispersed in different solvents under 375 nm excitation. As for phosphorescent emission, because the phosphorescence of CDs is often emitted in solid state rather than in solvent state, the influence of solvent relaxation effect on phosphorescent emission is still unclear.

The interaction between CDs and solvents is also a key factor affecting the optical properties of CDs. Li et al. [89] used water molecules to build a hydrogen bond network between CDs and CA to effectively promote phosphorescence. In the presence of water, the phosphorescent intensity of CD-CA is about three times higher than that of the dry CD-CA (Figure 18c), and the average lifetime increases from 0.253 s to 0.687 s. It is found that the bound water molecules between CDs and CA build a solid bridge like hydrogen bond network between them, which effectively hardens the C=O bond of CDs, and greatly enhances the stiffness of the whole system, thus promoting phosphorescent emission (Figure 18d).

A great deal of phosphorescent CDs often show sensitivity to pH, which is typically shown as the influence of pH on phosphorescent intensity and emission wavelength. This is usually related to the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of CDs. At the same time, the change of pH also affects the stability of related structures of CDs and the formation of hydrogen bonds, thereby affecting phosphorescent emission. The P-CDs designed and synthesized by Su et al. [15] contain abundant P–O–H functional groups on the surface under acidic conditions. Therefore, a large number of hydrogen bonds formed between P=O bonds and –OH ensure the stability of P-CDs under acidic conditions. With the addition of NaOH, the pH value increases continuously, and the deprotonation of the P–O on surface results in hydrogen bond dissociation, surface passivation and precipitation, which induce the weakening of phosphorescence intensity (Figure 18e, f). But in the study of Tan et al. [81], pH has an opposite effect on phosphorescence intensity (Figure 18g). High pH (11.5) results in deprotonation of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs. The deprotonation of carboxyl groups causes the increase of the negative charge on the surface of CDs, as demonstrated by zeta potential results. The produced negative charge shifts the Fermi level and inhibits the electron transition of some emission sites by occupying the energy levels. In addition, the deprotonation of carboxyl group weakens the electron absorption of carboxyl group to carbon core, and increases the degree of conjugation between carboxyl group and carbon core. The large degree of conjugation is more conducive to the generation of excited electrons. The increase of the number of excited electrons and the decrease of surface defects lead to strong steady-state PL and RTP emissions. Moreover, the strength of hydrogen bond interaction and the stability of CD-matrix composites are also affected by the pH value of the system, resulting in different RTP properties.

In the study of CDs, temperature always plays an important role. In the CDs synthesized by ethylenediamine and phosphoric acid, phosphorescence can be turned on by simple heating. Jiang et al. [51] used a hot air gun to heat the material without phosphorescent phenomenon to produce phosphorescence. The authors attributed the RTP to the more compact carbon core formed by the self-curing of structure caused by heating. The sub-fluorophore formed at a lower temperature leads to its fluorescent emission. After high temperature treatment, the originally wound polymer chains further crosslink with each other to form a more compact carbon core structure, effectively suppressing vibration and rotation, meanwhile avoiding the quenching of aggregation-induced sub-fluorophore. This process is irreversible. But in Liu's research [90], the phosphorescent phenomenon in CD/PVP system weakens with the increase of temperature, which is attributed to the increase of oxygen permeation rate with the increase of temperature, thus quenching the triplet exciton and deactivating the phosphorescence.

In the study of phosphorescence, there is an interesting phenomenon. There is a class of CDs, of which the phosphorescence intensity and even switch are determined by irradiation intensity and irradiation time. This magical phenomenon is named as "photo-induced RTP".

As shown in Figure 19 , Liu et al. [44,90] realized the new mode of image steganography by using the CD/PVP system with the property of photo-induced RTP, and further realized the gray-scale display of phosphorescent image (displaying a series of intensities with gradient) through the halogenation of CDs. Here, PVP acts both as a solid matrix and an oxygen reservoir, simultaneously providing hydrogen bonding fixation that facilitates the DURTP of CDs and triplet oxygen that suppresses its emission. CDs also play a dual role, both as an oxygen-sensitive phosphor and as an oxygen-consuming photosensitizer. The realization of DURTP is based on the fact that CDs transform the triplet oxygen into singlet oxygen in the photodynamic process during a long time of illumination, and the singlet oxygen is consumed owing to the presence of PVP (reductive macromolecules), thus realizing the photo-induced RTP phenomenon.

But in the study of traditional OLED phosphorescence, this phenomenon is attributed to the tighter packing mode and stronger π-π interaction in single crystals after the photodynamic process during a long time of illumination.

To summarize the previous work is to expect to get a universal law that can guide the future. In this review, the current regulation methods of phosphorescent CDs are summarized, so that we can get the universal laws of phosphorescent CD synthesis and regulation in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of phosphorescent QY and lifetime
ce:section.ce:para:
The factors affecting the QY and lifetime of phosphorescence have been widely studied, and now there are relatively clear conclusions. QY is referred to as the ratio of the number of photons emitted and the number of photons absorbed by light-emitting material. Phosphorescent lifetime is generally referred to as the time required for the phosphorescent intensity to decay to 1/e of its initial intensity. The two indices can be described by the following equations [36,91]:
τ p
=
1
k p
+
k n
Φ
p
=
k p
k p
+
k n
where τ p is the lifetime; and k p and k n the radiative recombination and nonradiative recombination rates, respectively.

Therefore, weakening k n is an effective method to extend phosphorescence lifetime and enhance intensity. Usually, various measures to inhibit the non-radiative transition of CDs, such as introducing host materials, heating, and curing, are adopted to enhance the phosphorescent intensity and lifetime. However, changing k p without changing k n, such as introducing halogen, metal elements, and other SOC units, usually increases QY while shortens lifetime.

Along with the general rules, there are also some special hybrid structures that can be used to prepare materials with ultralong afterglow lifetime, which have been introduced in 2.2 and are not be repeated here.

The phosphorescent wavelength is mostly determined by carbon core state and surface state, and the influencing factors are the same as those of fluorescent materials. In previous sections, reducing the size of CDs to achieve ultraviolet phosphorescent emission and preparing multi-color phosphorescence CDs with different oxidation degrees have been described. However, it is interesting and worth learning to use the energy transfer mechanism to realize long wavelength phosphorescent emission. Wang et al. [92] and Liang et al. [93] used Dexter and Fӧrster mechanisms to respectively realize the preparation and application of highly stable red-phosphorescent materials, where Dexter energy transfer is defined as the direct transfer of electrons or holes to other molecules to form new excitons, while Fӧrster energy transfer is referred to as the transfer of energy to other excitons in the form of light emission–reabsorption. Wang et al. prepared CDs@MnAPO-CJ50 by embedding CDs with higher triplet state into the matrix with octahedral Mn. The energy transfer efficiency of the composite reaches 97.5%. The material design is based on the selection of CDs with higher triplet energy levels, so that there is a larger energy transfer overlapping between the CDs and the matrix, thus improving the energy transfer efficiency. At the same time, octahedral Mn was selected as the red light-emitting receptor, and its closer distance to CDs was used to suppress its non-radiative transition to achieve red phosphorescence (Figure 20a ). Liang et al. used CDs as energy donor and RhB as energy acceptor. The good match between the phosphorescent emission spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of RhB achieves red phosphorescence through Fӧrster energy transfer (energy transfer efficiency up to 99.2%) (Figure 20b–d).

The ultimate goal of mechanism research is to guide the synthesis of CDs and expand their applications. Owing to their high optical stability, good biocompatibility, and simple preparation method, phosphorescent CDs are widely used in various fields from anti-counterfeiting to biological imaging, as well as in the field of detection, monitoring, and electroluminescent devices.
The afterglow characteristics and property diversity of CDs make them shine in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

There is a great diversity in the application of phosphorescent CDs in the field of anti-counterfeiting, including encoding anti-counterfeiting with full-color phosphorescent CDs [59,74,75]. There are also time-dependent color changing multiple anti-counterfeiting materials that take advantage of the multiple luminous centers of CDs, which can be classified into two categories. The first type of dynamic color changing phosphorescence is realized by using the dual emission centers of CDs [82]. Another type of dynamic color changing phosphorescence is achieved by using anti-counterfeiting materials [94] that combine CDs and matrix as composite materials. And the emission centers of CDs and host materials can be activated at the same time. Then, there are multiple anti-counterfeiting applications that take advantage of the multi-factor sensitivity of phosphorescent CDs [51,76]. At last, there are gray-scale display materials designed by DURTP for anti-counterfeiting [44].

The anti-interference, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and large Stokes shift of phosphorescent CDs with following characteristics—low background interference, strong penetrability, little damage to biological samples, and high detection sensitivity—make CDs suitable for biological imaging.

Li et al. [18] used phosphorescent CDs to achieve clear imaging in mung bean sprouts and mouse breast cancer cells, showing the potential of CDs to image in living cells. Liang et al. [93] achieve in vivo bioimaging through CDs-RhB@silica. As indicated in Figure 21a
[95], CDs-DNA1 synthesized by CDs and DNA1 emit green phosphorescence under the excitation of UV light. 5Fu-DNA2 synthesized by 5Fu and DNA2 pairs with DNA1 to make the excitation energy of CDs transfer to 5Fu through photo-induced electron transfer, thus quenching phosphorescence, and double stranded complementary DNA is used as the carrier of Glu. When DNA2, as the aptamer of AFP, specifically recognizes AFP, DNA spins off, resuming phosphorescence CDs and releasing Glu. This design shows the potential of phosphorescent CDs as a new biological probe.
The sensitivity of many factors makes phosphorescence CDs widely used in the field of detection and monitoring, including the detection of metal ions and other substances.

The Fӧrster energy transfer between phosphorescent CDs and tested substance can quench phosphorescence. Therefore, using this mechanism can realize the on-off of phosphorescence for substance detection. Compared with traditional fluorescence detection methods, phosphorescent CDs have a dual channel detection mode. As shown in Figure 21b, warfarin quenches the phosphorescence of CDs@creatine meanwhile enhancing its fluorescence [96]. Lu et al. [97] constructed a ratio phosphorescent probe through Mn-doped ZnSe quantum dots and CDs for thallium detection. The phosphorescence of Mn-doped ZnSe quantum dots is selectively quenched by thallium, while the phosphorescence emission of CDs remains unchanged.

The tunability, environmental friendliness, and broad emission spectra of CDs make them perform well in LEDs. These devices can be divided into photoluminescence type and electroluminescence type according to the way achieving luminescence. Because phosphorescent luminescence mechanism is different from fluorescence one, it is expected to show a far greater application prospect in the direction of electroluminescent devices than fluorescent CDs.

Photoluminescent devices [25,38,27,98] are mainly prepared by embedding phosphorescent CDs into solid matrices (organic silicon [27], PVP [25], etc.). Generally, white phosphorescent devices made by this method usually own high color rendering index, and the emitted light is extremely close to pure white light. Shi et al. [69] used CDs with good conductivity and solvent processability to prepare electroluminescent red phosphorescent devices with an EQE (5.6%) higher than 5% (the theoretical limit of fluorescent electroluminescent LED devices). A facile spin coating method can be used to prepare the luminescent layer, negating the introduction of other matrix materials (Figure 21c).
Finally, we summarize the application directions of common phosphorescent CDs in addition to anti-counterfeiting in Table 2 .

In this paper, research progress of phosphorescent CDs is reviewed from two aspects: researching mechanisms of promoting the phosphorescent emission of CDs and exploring factors affecting the phosphorescent emission of CDs. Research into mechanisms that promote the phosphorescent emission of CDs is combined with research into traditional organic phosphorescent luminogens. The mechanisms of phosphorescence emission can be divided into two aspects: the enhancement of the ISC process and the stabilization of the triplet exciton. Clarifying the process of ISC enhancement is mainly describing the influence caused by the doping of different SOC units. The construction of common n electron SOC units and interdot CT states has been widely used as a common means, but there is still no research on intra-dot CT states. In terms of the stabilization of triplet exciton, rather than using an external rigid environment or forming a rigid environmental confinement by the interaction between CDs, there is still a unique self-protection mechanism in CDs. This review summarizes and analyses the strength of the force acting on CDs. According to the existing research, the use of covalent interactions as strong interactions results in significantly better phosphorescent property (higher quantum yield). However, compared with covalent interactions, ion interactions are rarely investigated. Hopefully there will be more studies on this in the future, and we can try to build intra-dot CT states by drawing on the research experience of small organic molecules.

In the process of exploring the factors that affect the phosphorescent emission of CDs, four models are involved: carbon core state, surface state, synergistic effects between them, and environmental factors. For the carbon core state, the size effect is mainly considered. The concept of surface state is introduced when the change of energy level in CDs is considered. The synergistic effect between carbon core state and surface state combines them together to produce more phosphorescent carbon dot materials. At present, research on the surface state of phosphorescent CDs focuses more on regulating the energy level and promoting phosphorescence emission, while research on phosphorescence regulation can only learn from related research on fluorescence emission of CDs. The interesting interaction between CDs and their external environment often produces unexpected effects, resulting in various interesting phenomena. Among the various environmental effects, the solvent effect of common phosphorescent CDs has not been well studied owing to their poor solvent processability.

Elucidation of the mechanism would provide guidance for material synthesis. Therefore, this review briefly summarized how to tune phosphorescent CDs from two aspects of phosphorescence intensity and lifetime, and phosphorescent wavelength. However, at present, the synthesis of phosphorescent materials from two aspects can only be achieved in theory. The accurate regulation of material properties has not yet been achieved, which requires more in-depth and quantitative research to establish an accurate structure–property relationship in the future.

The ultimate purpose of research into properties and mechanisms is to guide production and application. Various properties of phosphorescent CDs enrich their application scenarios. Their afterglow characteristics and sensitivity to various external environmental factors make them shine in the field of anti-counterfeiting. Their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and anti-interference make them widely applicable in the field of biological imaging. At the same time, their sensitivity to various external factors makes them brilliant in the sensing field. Finally, owing to the difference between phosphorescence and fluorescence emission mechanism, a theoretical 100% exciton utilization is expected in electroluminescence, which makes them have excellent application prospects in the field of electroluminescence. Further in-depth study of phosphorescence mechanism will enrich their application.

It is worth emphasizing again that the ultimate purpose of studying the phosphorescence emission mechanism of CDs is to guide production and application, and the core of this process is structure. Exploring phosphorescence emission mechanism of CDs can guide their structural design, which is essential for production and application. In order to achieve a specific application purpose, it is necessary to design a specific structure on the basis of the deep insight of its phosphorescence mechanism. Nowadays, a systematic study of the structure (carbon core and surface state) requires better characterization measures and better research methods. Only a clear understanding of the structures of CDs can clarify their phosphorescence mechanism and enrich their applications. Although the road ahead is bumpy, great progress will be made in this field. We hope that in the future, researchers will be able to conduct more sophisticated and novel research to enrich the achievements in this field.
